content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 24,
1994
Glenn A. Miller
7990 Moss Street
Fair Oaks,
CA
95628
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-94-068
Dear Mr. Miller:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning potential conflict—of—interest situations which may
arise under the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) 1
Since your
advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
but instead seeks general guidance,
we are treating your request
as one for informal assistance.
2
QUESTION
1.
Because of your position as an employee of the Fair Oaks
Water District, are you restricted from participating in
performance evaluations as well as salary and benefit adjustments
for the position of the General Manager of the Fair Oaks Water
District while acting in your capacity as a member of the Board of
Directors of the Fair Oaks Water District?
2.
If you are elected to the office of Director of the San
Juan Suburban Water District will your present position as a
Director of the Fair Oaks Water District create a conflict of
interest?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—94-068
Page
2
CONCLUSION
1.
So long as the General Manager of the Fair Oaks Water
District is not an economic interest of yours,
as defined in
Section 87103 of the Act, the Act would not restrict you from
participating in the General Manager’s performance evaluations as
well as salary and benefit adjustments.
2.
Holding positions with the Fair Oaks Water District and
the San Juan Suburban Water District simultaneously will not
create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.
However, it would be advisable to contact the Attorney General’s
Office with respect to the doctrine of “incompatible offices” and
other provisions of law that might apply to your facts.
DISCUSSION
Incompatible Offices
The Act does not prohibit a public official from holding
multiple public positions, either within a single agency or
different agencies.
The conflict—of—interest provisions of the
Act (Section 87100 et g., discussed below)
prohibit a public
official from participating in a decision that will have a
financial effect on his or her economic interests.
However, this
prohibition is applied on a decision-by-decision basis,
and
several exceptions apply.
Please note that other laws, outside the jurisdiction of the
Commission,
do restrict the ability of public officials to hold
two different public offices simultaneously if the offices are
determined to be “incompatible offices.”
It would, therefore,
be
advisable to contact the Attorney General’s Office with respect to
the doctrine as applied to both of your questions.
Conflicts of Interest
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on,
among other things:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***


Our File No.
1—94—068
Page
3
(C)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a),
(C)
and (d).
For example, your employment with a business entity would
result in the business entity becoming an economic interest of
yours.
However, Section 82005 defines “business entity” as an
organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but not
limited to a proprietorship, partnership,
firm, business trust,
joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association.
Since a
local government agency is not an organization or enterprise
operated for profit, it is, therefore, not a “business entity” as
defined by the Act.
Thus, your position with the Fair Oaks Water
District will not result in the district becoming an economic
interest of yours under Section 87103(a)
or (d).
(Also see Darcy
Advice Letter,
No.
1-87—296.)
However, entities also become economic interests of an
official if they are sources of income to the official.
For
example, if you received income from a person or business entity,
the source of the income would be an economic interest of yours.
(Section 87103 (c).)
Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
diem received from a state, local or federal government agency,
however, are not regarded as income for purposes of the Act.
(Section 82030(b) (2).)
Absent some other disqualifying financial interest as set
forth in Section 87103, or some direct financial effect on you
personally resulting from a governmental decision, you will not
have a conflict of interest under the Act if you are elected to
the San Juan Suburban Water District due to your position with the
Fair Oaks Water District.
(Sampson Advice Letter,
No. 1-89-196.)
However, please note, as stated above, our advice is limited to
the Political Reform Act.


Our File No.
1-94—068
Page
4
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC:ld

one thousand dollars
